title: Facial Recognition Bias Audit Stimulus A
front: Student-facing stimulus 1: Audit row: hallway camera, 94% overall accuracy, but higher false positives in low light. Rank, sort, or audit this output. Identify which rule, ranking signal, or interface choice shaped your judgement.
back: Students inspect this exact card first and commit to a claim before hearing anyone else.
teacher_use: Print or project as the first shared stimulus.

title: Facial Recognition Bias Audit Stimulus B
front: Student-facing stimulus 2: Stakeholder card: student wrongly flagged, administrator managing safety, developer defending the model. Rank, sort, or audit this output. Identify which rule, ranking signal, or interface choice shaped your judgement.
back: Use this for comparison, a second round, or a partner challenge.
teacher_use: Reveal after the first response so students can revise.

title: Facial Recognition Bias Audit Reveal / Twist
front: Student-facing stimulus 3: Stakeholder card: student wrongly flagged, administrator managing safety, developer defending the model. Rank, sort, or audit this output. Identify which rule, ranking signal, or interface choice shaped your judgement.
back: Teacher reveal: connect the changed response to the big idea — Automated recognition is a knowledge claim whose reliability can vary across contexts and groups.
teacher_use: Use to create the revision moment.

title: Question 1
front: Write one claim in a complete sentence.
back: Teacher cue: look for a clear claim, not just a description.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 2
back: Teacher cue: students should name visible words, data, source features, method features, or contextual clues.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 3
back: Teacher cue: this is where assumptions, framing, models, or perspective usually appear.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 4
front: Circle one confidence level and justify it.
back: Teacher cue: confidence is data for the debrief, not a grade.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 5
front: Name one piece of missing information.
back: Teacher cue: push for method, source, context, comparison, or definitions.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Question 6
front: Answer using the activity as evidence.
back: Teacher cue: students should move from the classroom example to a general TOK issue.
teacher_use: Students answer before the teacher reveal.

title: Student Task
front: Use the stimulus cards to complete the observation/inference/evidence/confidence grid, then answer one TOK knowledge question connected to Knowledge and Technology.
back: When is a technological classification reliable enough to act on?
teacher_use: Use as the main activity instruction.

title: Teacher Reveal
front: The key move is not the “right answer”; it is the moment students see how automated recognition is a knowledge claim whose reliability can vary across contexts and groups.
back: Strong response: separates observation from inference. | Strong response: names a method, source, model, language choice, context, or perspective. | Strong response: revises confidence when new evidence or a new criterion appears. | Weak response: gives only opinion or says “it depends” without criteria.
teacher_use: Use after students commit to an answer.
